This line of storms produced three to six inches of rain over the city causing flash flooding leading to numerous water rescues.  Additional thunderstorms developed across the rest of southern Missouri.  A few of these storms became severe with wind gusts in excess of 60 mph and hail up to the size of golf balls.
Large thunderstorm complex over Iowa moved south into northern Missouri.  Isolated reports of damaging winds were received with some of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown over onto some power lines in Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of severe thunderstorms, rumbled across the area during the evening hours into the early morning hours, of March 23rd through 24th, 2009. There were numerous reports of hail.
A strong cold front moved south across the region, triggering strong to severe thunderstorms. Several tornadoes were reported, as well as large hail and damaging winds.
A cold front moved into northern Missouri triggering some isolated severe thunderstorms with large hail and heavy rain.
Isolated severe thunderstorms, produced large hail across extreme northwest Missouri, during the afternoon hours of August 19, 2009.

A cold front moved through the Mid-South during the early morning hours of July 26th, 2009. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front. The showers and storms continued into the morning hours before dying out. Some of the storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated at 60 mph 2 miles south of Cardwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms impacted extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks.  These storms developed along and near a warm front that surged into the region.  Large hail, damaging winds, and one tornado resulted from these storms.  Flash flooding was also observed in many areas of southern Missouri.
A large mesoscale convective system moved east across southern Missouri and southern Illinois causing widespread wind damage, as well as hail and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding.   Several roads were closed including U.S. Highway 67 north of Bonne Terre. Also, some ties were washed out from under train tracks just south of Bismarck and several water rescues were performed in the Park Hills area.  Two homes in Park Hills had up to 8 feet of water in their basements due to the heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over southwest Missouri during the early morning hours of 30 June.  Large hail and damaging winds were observed with these storms.  Additional severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of 30 June.  These storms also produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated 60 mph wind gusts near Crane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms impacted extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks.  These storms developed along and near a warm front that surged into the region.  Large hail, damaging winds, and one tornado resulted from these storms.  Flash flooding was also observed in many areas of southern Missouri.
A small supercell thunderstorm produce hail and wind damage across portions of south central Missouri.

Thunderstorms produced heavy rains across portions of northwest Missouri on may 15, 2009. Numerous flash flood warnings were needed. There were also reports of large hail. Rainfall amounts were as high as 10 inches in spots. This caused several area rivers to go into flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused 64th Street and North Baggett Street to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms produced damaging winds, hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missouri Department of Transportation closed Route U due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex moved through northeast Missouri and west central Illinois.  Some of the storms produced large hail.
A strong upper level storm system, caused severe thunderstorms on April 26, 2009. There were several reports of high winds and hail, across Nodaway, Andrew, and Livingston counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A billboard was blown over at the intersection of Highway 65 and Highway 190.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized complex of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front as it approached southern Illinois and southeast Missouri during the hottest and most unstable time of day. Very strong instability, coupled with moderately strong mid-level winds, resulted in a few damaging wind events with the initial complex that affected places east of a Dexter to Marble Hill line. In the wake of the initial complex, multi-cell clusters rapidly developed on the upshear side of the cold pool left by the first complex. These clusters produced several large hail events, along with isolated damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 58 mph was measured by a spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense squall line impacted extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks with mainly damaging winds. However, 19 tornadoes along with large hail was also observed. Due to the straight line nature of the winds, damage was widespread and intense.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty to 85 mph wind gusts created widespread damge to trees, power poles, and a few structures across much of Dade County.  The southern half of the county was impacted the greatest, particularly in the community of Lockwood where numerous large trees were uprooted.  Power line damage created power outages for residents of the community.  There was also considerable tree and power pole damage in Greenfield as several city streets were blocked by debris.  Power outages in Greenfield ranged from 24 to 48 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms, brought damaging winds to Clay County, late |in the afternoon and evening hours, of June 23, 2009.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The top of a 15 foot tall tree was snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense squall line impacted extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks with mainly damaging winds. However, 19 tornadoes along with large hail was also observed. Due to the straight line nature of the winds, damage was widespread and intense.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty to 80 mph wind gusts impacted all of Oregon County.  Significant tree damage was observed countywide, while numerous outbuildings and homes sustained damage.  It was relayed to the National Weather Service that several homes in the community of Koshkonong had windows that were blown out.  Roof damage was widespread in all communities of Oregon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense squall line impacted extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks with mainly damaging winds. However, 19 tornadoes along with large hail was also observed. Due to the straight line nature of the winds, damage was widespread and intense.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey revealed that an EF-1 tornado touched down approximately two miles northwest of Brighton, and tracked north across southern Polk County.  The tornado lifted approximately two miles east southeast of Bolivar.  The tornado damaged or destroyed several outbuildings and barns, and caused moderate to severe damage to frame homes.  In addtion, numerous trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed in a very unstable air mass to the west of a stationary front that extended from southwest Indiana into Mid Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were brought down. A trained spotter estimated winds gusted to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms along a slow moving cold front produced large hail, damaging winds, heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 38 was closed due to flooding along the Hawk River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms along a slow moving cold front produced heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route E was closed south of State Route 142 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rainfall and flooding occurred across southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks. The heavy rainfall was associated with several rounds of efficient rain producing stratiform events along a stalled warm front. Isolated convective elements embedded in the system and a few ahead of the system also produce some enhanced rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missouri Department of Transportation reported flooding on State Highway D southwest of Preston and on State Highway O southwest of Wheatland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary across the area, generated severe thunderstorms with large hail and high winds, late in the night into the early morning hours, of March 7th and 8th, 2009.
Isolated thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of May 13th.

A large complex of thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary as it moved south across the region.  Numerous reports of large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding were received.  Also, two brief tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous large tree limbs east of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms dumped up to 3 inches of rain over northern portions of Franklin county causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding.  Several roads were flooded including Fiddle Creek Road and Little Tavern Road north of Pacific.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system moved through the region triggering showers and thunderstorms. There were isolated reports of large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding.  The River Des Peres as well as smaller creeks were out of their banks flooding some roads and homes in the area, especially along Wilson Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms caused marginal flooding over St. Clair County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused six low water crossings across the county to become flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms impacted portions of southwest and central Missouri.  The primary hazard with these storms were severe wind gusts that caused damage to trees, power poles, and a few structures.  Two weak tornadoes also occurred from these storms.

Numerous severe thunderstorms impacted extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks.  These storms developed along and near a warm front that surged into the region.  Large hail, damaging winds, and one tornado resulted from these storms.  Flash flooding was also observed in many areas of southern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed a large tree within Cassville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rains across portions of northwest Missouri on may 15, 2009. Numerous flash flood warnings were needed. There were also reports of large hail. Rainfall amounts were as high as 10 inches in spots. This caused several area rivers to go into flood.

On the 28th, a cold front sagged south across the area and stalled across Tennessee. Numerous rounds of showers and isolated thunderstorms produced rain amounts between 1 and 3 inches. This forced the St. Francis River above flood stage again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the St. Francis River occurred near Patterson. Low-lying fields and bottomlands near the river were flooded. At the Patterson gage, where flood stage is 16 feet, the river crested at 16.27 feet around 3:45 A.M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms increased along an axis from central Arkansas into southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. These storms moved through a region of marginal instability, but deep-layer vertical wind shear was sufficient for isolated severe storms with rotation. The complex of storms contained very heavy rainfall that resulted in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads were impassable in Sikeston. Some motorists were stranded. At Diehlstadt, a spotter measured 3.27 inches of rain in a period of less than two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system, combined with rich gulf moisture, helped to produce several reports of hail and an isolated tornado, in the late afternoon and evening hours of April 25, 2009.
A cold front moved through the region triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced severe weather including large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large sycamores were uprooted. Both were at least 2 and a half feet in diameter.  Also, a three foot diameter oak tree was uprooted and fell onto a house causing moderate damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain over much of the Mississippi River Valley caused many rivers to rise above flood stage. May continued the active pattern begun in April. Several of these events produced very heavy rainfall. Rainfall was one to two inches above normal in most spots for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Mississippi River occurred for most of the month of May. At the New Madrid gage, where flood stage is 34 feet, the river crested at 39.11 feet on the 15th. Agricultural bottomland and low-lying areas were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions were in place across the region on June 23.  A line of showers and storms plowed east and southeast across eastern Iowa, northeastern Missouri, and west central Illinois. Some of the storms were severe packing winds of 60 to 80 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be 60 mph blew down some tree branches about 3 miles northeast of Bible Grove, MO around 715 pm June 23. The branches fell onto some power lines and caught on fire.
